name: generics
context: TypeScript generics — type parameters, constraints, inference, conditional types, and reusable type-safe abstractions
info: Generics are TypeScript's mechanism for writing reusable, type-safe code that works with multiple types without losing type information. A generic function or type accepts type parameters (like `<T>`) that are filled in at each usage site, either explicitly or via type inference. Generics are essential for agent development: they power typed API clients, type-safe event emitters, generic data structures, and framework abstractions. This node covers generic functions, generic interfaces and classes, type parameter constraints (`extends`), default type parameters, the `infer` keyword for extracting types, conditional types with generics, variance annotations (`in`/`out`), and common generic patterns like builder types and fluent APIs. See [[typescript/types]] for the underlying type system and [[typescript/config]] for strict mode options that affect generic inference.

sections.title: Generic functions
sections.body: Generic functions accept type parameters in angle brackets: `function fn<T>(arg: T): T`. TypeScript infers `T` from the argument when possible, or you can specify it explicitly: `fn<string>("hello")`. Arrow functions in `.tsx` files need a trailing comma to disambiguate from JSX: `<T,>(x: T) => x`. Use multiple type parameters when inputs/outputs must correlate: `function map<T, U>(arr: T[], fn: (item: T) => U): U[]`. Generic functions enable typed wrappers, data transformations, and API clients that preserve request/response type relationships.

sections.title: Type parameter constraints
sections.body: Use `extends` to restrict generic parameters: `<T extends { length: number }>` ensures `T` has a `length` property. Common constraint patterns: `<K extends keyof T>` (key of an object type), `<T extends readonly unknown[]>` (any array/tuple), `<T extends (...args: any[]) => any>` (any function), `<T extends new (...args: any[]) => any>` (any constructor). Constraints enable safe property access and method calls on generic values. Default parameters work with constraints: `<T extends object = Record<string, unknown>>`.

sections.title: The infer keyword
sections.body: The `infer` keyword declares a type variable inside a conditional type's `extends` clause: `T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T` extracts the promised type. Common uses: `T extends (infer E)[]` (array element), `T extends (...args: infer P) => infer R` (function parts), `T extends { [key: string]: infer V }` (object value type). `infer` can appear in template literal types: `` T extends `${infer Head}-${infer Tail}` ``. Multiple `infer` positions in the same clause extract multiple types. `infer` is the building block for TypeScript's built-in utility types like `ReturnType`, `Parameters`, and `Awaited`.

sections.title: Variance annotations
sections.body: Variance describes how subtyping of a generic type relates to subtyping of its type parameter. `out T` (covariant): if `Dog extends Animal`, then `Producer<Dog> extends Producer<Animal>`. `in T` (contravariant): if `Dog extends Animal`, then `Consumer<Animal> extends Consumer<Dog>`. `in out T` (invariant): no subtype relationship. TypeScript 4.7+ lets you annotate variance explicitly. This improves type safety (compiler catches misuse) and compilation speed (compiler skips structural comparison). Use `out` for types that produce/return `T`, `in` for types that consume/accept `T`.

sections.title: Common generic patterns
sections.body: **Event emitter**: `class Emitter<Events extends Record<string, unknown>>` with `on<K extends keyof Events>(event: K, handler: (data: Events[K]) => void)`. **Builder**: methods return `this` for chaining, with generic constraints on keys. **Branded types**: `type Brand<T, B> = T & { __brand: B }` creates nominally distinct types from structural equivalents. **Type-safe records**: `Record<K, V>` with constrained keys. **Discriminated union factories**: generic functions that return discriminated unions based on input. **Recursive types**: `type DeepPartial<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: DeepPartial<T[K]> }` for deep transformations.
